question_text: Applying collimation to an x-ray beam will do what to image contrast?
explanation: Collimation reduces the area of the x-ray beam. Reduced beam area decreases the amount of scattered radiation that can potentially reach the image receptor. By reducing scatter, collimation should increase or improve image contrast. Collimation will also reduce patient dose.

question_text: In an x-ray imaging system with a uniform x-ray exposure, if the mean number of photons incident on the detector is 10000 photons per square mm, what is the relative standard deviation?
explanation: The relative standard deviation is the standard deviation (here, the square root of 10,000, or 100) divided by the mean (10,000), or 1%.

question_text: Which of the following imaging modalities has the highest spatial resolution?
explanation: Spatial resolution describes the ability of an imaging modality to distinguish small adjacent objects as distinct separate objects. It is measured in line pairs per mm. Digital Mammography (7-10 lp/mm) > Computed Radiography (3 lp/mm) > CT (1 |p/mm) > MRI (0.3 lp/mm) > PET (0.1 |p/mm)

question_text: How does decreasing the size of the focal spot affect spatial resolution?
explanation: The smaller the focal spot, the sharper the image that is produced. Therefore, spatial resolution improves as the size of the focal spot decreases. This holds true for plain film radiography, mammography, and CT imaging. Mammography tubes have focal spots of 0.1 and 0.3 mm while general diagnostic tubes typically sizes of 0.6 mm to 1.2mm.

question_text: When interacting with atoms, x-rays are most likely to transfer energy to which subatomic particle?
explanation: X-rays primarily interact with and transfer energy to electrons. These energy transfers may result in an ionization event, such as a photoelectric or Compton interaction.

question_text: For incident photon energies (E) above the k-edge, the probability of a photoelectric absorption is proportional to:
explanation: For incident photon energies (E) above the k-edge, the probability of a photoelectric absorption is proportional to 1/E“3. This means that the probability of photoelectric absorption decrease dramatically as the photon energy increases over the k-edge.

question_text: The heel effect is not dependent on which of the following?
explanation: The magnitude of the heel effect primarily depends on three factors: 1) anode angle, 2) source-to-image detector distance, and 3) field size. The heel effect increases with decreasing anode angle, decreasing source-to-image detector distance, and increasing field size.

question_text: Which one of the following statements regarding Compton scatter is correct?
choices: A. Interactions involve incident photons and inner shell atomic electrons
B. Probability of Compton scattering is proportional to the electron density of the tissue
C. Wavelength and direction of the scattered photon are unchanged
D. Probability of Compton scattering is directly proportional to incident photon energy
correct_answer_text: B. Probability of Compton scattering is proportional to the electron density of the tissue
explanation: Compton scatter interactions involve incident photons and outer shell atomic electrons. The wavelength and direction of the incident photon are changed by a Compton scatter interaction. The probability of Compton scatter is proportional to the electron density of the tissue and inversely proportional to the incident photon energy.

question_text: What is the relationship between x-ray energy and tissue contrast?
choices: A. Lower mean x-ray photon energy = lower tissue contrast
B. X-ray photon energy and tissue contrast are not related
C. Lower mean x-ray photon energy = higher tissue contrast
correct_answer_text: C. Lower mean x-ray photon energy = higher tissue contrast
explanation: The lower the mean x-ray photon energy, the higher the tissue contrast.

question_text: If the x-ray tube current is halved and the exposure time is doubled, which of the following is the effect on the x-ray beam intensity?
choices: B. Increased by a factor of 4
C. Decreased by a factor of 16
D. Increased by a factor of 16
E. Decreased by a factor of 4
explanation: X-ray beam intensity is linearly related to tube current and exposure time; so in this scenario, the changes cancel out and the intensity is unchanged.

question_text: Increasing the frequency of an incident photon will result in which of the following?
explanation: For electromagnetic radiation, velocity = wavelength x frequency. The velocity of a photon is constant at the speed of light. Thus, if the frequency increases, then the wavelength must decrease. Also, Energy = Planck’s constant X (speed of light / wavelength). The energy of a photon is directly proportional to its frequency and indirectly proportional to its wavelength. In other words, long wavelength or low frequency photons (e.g. radio waves) carry less energy than short wavelength or high frequency photons (e.g. microwave or UV).

question_text: What effect will reducing x-ray tube voltage ripple have on tissue contrast?
explanation: Reducing tube voltage ripple will increase the average x-ray photon energy, which in turn will decrease tissue contrast. Current supplied by the power company is called AC or alternating current which flows in one direction, then the other. Rectifier circuits change AC current into a current that flows in one direction only but result in a voltage ripple. Combining multiple rectifier circuits gives you a current that more closely resembles and constant value or DC/direct current by reducing the ripple effect.

question_text: How is the power (P) dissipated by an x-ray tube related to current (I) and voltage (V)?
explanation: The power dissipated by an x-ray tube is calculated using Joule's Law: Power = Voltage x Current (or P = IV). This means that the amount of power deposited on the Tungsten target is directly proportional to the tube voltage (keV) and the tube current (mAs). Power is expressed in Watts, which has the units Joules/sec or energy/time.

question_text: Which of the following materials has the smallest half-value layer?
explanation: Lead, having the highest atomic number of the materials listed, has the shortest half-value layer (HVL). HVL = amount of material required to attenuate 50% of an X-ray beam. Also remember: as the average energy of an X-ray beam increases, the HVL increases.

question_text: What should the average energy of the x-ray beam be to optimize visualization of the iodinated contrast?
explanation: The K-edge of iodine is 33.2 keV. To optimize visualization of the iodinated contrast, you want to maximize the probability of a photoelectric interaction/absorption. Therefore, the average keV of the incident photon beam should be just above the K-edge of iodine. Photons with energies less than the K-edge are unable to displace the requisite inner shell electron and photoelectric absorption cannot occur. Higher energies are not optimal since the likelihood of the photoelectric effect decreases dramatically as the photon energy increases above the K-edge. Therefore, an average energy of 35 keV would be the best choice. Remember: the average energy of an x-ray beam is 1/3 to 1/2 of the maximum energy (kVp), so the kVp of the beam should be 70 kV.

question_text: Which one of the following statements regarding focal spot sizes is correct?
choices: A. Large focal spots are preferred when imaging with short exposure times
B. The larger the anode angle (the angle between the target surface and the incident electrons), the smaller the focal spot
C. Large focal spots provide sharper images compared to small focal spots
D. Small focal spots result in less heat generation compared to large focal spots
correct_answer_text: A. Large focal spots are preferred when imaging with short exposure times
explanation: Large focal spots result in less heat generation than small focal spots. As a result, if the imaging technique requires short exposure times and therefore high tube currents, a larger focal spot must be used. The larger the anode angle (the angle between the target surface and a vertical line orthogonal to the electron beam), the larger the focal spot. Small focal spots provide better spatial resolution by decreasing geometric distortion at the image detector. The focal spot size has no effect relative to image contrast or patient dose.

question_text: How will increasing atomic number (Z) affect the probability of a photoelectric interaction/absorption?
explanation: The probability of a photoelectric absorption increases dramatically with increasing atomic number (Z) and is proportional to Z^3. It is inversely proportional to the energy cubed of the incoming photon. To increase photoelectric absorption (which affects image contrast) requires an increase in Z and/or a decrease in photon energy (as is the case with mammography).

question_text: A radiation absorbed dose of 1 Gray is equal to which of the following?
explanation: Radiation doses are expressed in Grays, which has the units of Joules/kg or energy/mass. Power is expressed in Watts, which has the units Joules/sec or energy/time.

question_text: Which type of magnification results in increased patient dose?
explanation: Both geometric and electronic magnification will increase the patient dose. However, electronic magnification increases patient dose to a lesser degree than geometric magnification.

question_text: The x-ray tube anode dissipates a large amount of heat energy primarily by which method?
explanation: Anodes primarily release heat energy by radiating light photons.

question_text: Which is not a process by which x-ray photons interact with matter?
explanation: Compton scatter, photoelectric effect, and Rayleigh scatter (also known as coherent scatter) are processes by which high-energy photons interact with matter. Bremsstrahlung is a process by which X-rays are produced when electrons are accelerated into an anode target.

question_text: Which of the following has the highest K-shell binding energy?
explanation: Lead has the highest atomic number (Z) = highest K-shell binding energy

question_text: Which imaging modality has the worst soft tissue contrast?
explanation: Computed radiography has the worst soft tissue contrast. MRI > CT > Digital Mammography > Computed Radiography. Contrast is the ability to distinguish two soft tissue lesions of slightly varying composition. The contrast of digital mammography is better than computed radiography, primarily due to the lower kVp values used to perform digital mammography. Mammography requires more soft tissue contrast in order to distinguish dense cancers from a normal background of glandular soft tissue. The soft tissue contrast of MRI is better than CT.

question_text: Which one of the following statements regarding the photoelectric effect is correct?
choices: A. Occurs between outer shell electrons and incident x-rays
B. Probability of a photoelectric interaction decreases sharply when the incident x-ray energy falls below the K-shell electron binding energy
C. Probability of a photoelectric interaction decreases with increasing atomic number (Z)
D. Results in partial absorption of the incident x-ray energy
correct_answer_text: B. Probability of a photoelectric interaction decreases sharply when the incident x-ray energy falls below the K-shell electron binding energy
explanation: The probability of a photoelectric interaction decreases sharply when the incident x-ray energy falls below the K-shell electron binding energy. This is because the incident x-ray energy is no longer great enough to displace the K-shell electron. Photoelectric effect results in complete absorption of the incident x-ray energy. As a result, no scatter radiation is produced by the interaction. In other words, photoelectric absorption can only occur when the photon energy exceeds the electron shell binding energy, with increased probability just above the shell binding energy. Increasing the atomic number (Z) of a material will increase the probability of a photoelectric effect interaction by a factor of Z^3.

question_text: Which one of the following statements regarding Compton scatter is correct?
choices: A. A photon may undergo multiple Compton scatter interactions
B. It accounts for a minority of scatter interactions between x-rays and tissue
C. Photons that are backscattered (180 degree scatter) retain more energy than photons that are scattered at low angles
D. It is a non-ionizing interaction, as no atomic electrons are ejected
E. In most soft tissues, it is the dominant effect over the photoelectric effect for x-rays with energies less than 25 keV
correct_answer_text: A. A photon may undergo multiple Compton scatter interactions
explanation: Compton scatter is an ionizing interaction, where an outer shell electron is ejected. Compton scatter accounts for the vast majority of scatter interactions. Backscattered photons transfer the most energy to the ejected electron and retain less energy than those that scatter at low angles. At energies less than 25 keV, the photoelectric effect dominates over the Compton effect. Above 25 keV, the Compton effect dominates.

question_text: Which of the following statements regarding Auger electrons is true?
choices: A. Auger electron energy = characteristic X-ray energy - binding energy of the released outer shell electron
B. They are inner shell electrons that completely absorb energy from an incident electron and are ejected from the atom
C. Generation of characteristic x-rays always results in the release of an Auger electron
D. Inner K-shell electron can be ejected only if the incident electron energy is exactly equal to the K-shell electron's binding energy
correct_answer_text: A. Auger electron energy = characteristic X-ray energy - binding energy of the released outer shell electron
explanation: The photoelectric effect refers to when an inner shell electron completely absorbs energy from an incident photon, and the electron is ejected from the inner shell of the atom. An inner K-shell electron can be ejected if the incident x-ray energy is greater than or equal to the binding energy of the electron. After the inner K-shell electron is ejected, the K-shell vacancy must be filled. The K-shell vacancy is filled by an outer shell electron. This results in a release of energy equal to the binding energy difference between the inner and outer shells. The energy can be released in two possible forms. One possibility is that this energy is released as a characteristic x-ray. Characteristic x-rays are important for mammographic imaging. A second possibility is that the energy is transferred to another outer shell electron, which is then ejected from the outer shell. The ejected outer shell electron is termed an Auger electron. The energy of an Auger electron is equal to the difference between the characteristic x-ray energy minus the binding energy of the released outer shell electron.

question_text: What is relationship between wavelength, frequency, and velocity?
choices: B. Velocity = speed of light x frequency / wavelength
explanation: Velocity = frequency x wavelength. For electromagnetic waves, velocity is constant = speed of light. Also remember: Energy = Planck’s constant x (speed of light / wavelength). Frequency = (speed of light / wavelength). Therefore, E = Planck’s constant x Frequency

question_text: What does the Bucky factor indicate?
choices: A. Surface area coverage by the grid
B. Improved resolution with grid use
C. Improved contrast with grid use
D. Increased dose with grid use
correct_answer_text: D. Increased dose with grid use
explanation: The Bucky factor represents the factor by which the radiation dose increases with use of a grid. The Bucky factor is simply a ratio of the dose with a grid to the dose without a grid.

question_text: Which type of interaction is least likely to reduce image contrast?
explanation: Contrast is primarily degraded by scatter. Photoelectric interactions are not associated with any scatter, so these would not reduce image contrast. The other two interactions result in scatter. Compton scatter is the primary source of scatter radiation in radiography, and coherent scatter contributes to a lesser degree.

question_text: What is the name of the process by which x-rays are produced?
explanation: The Bremsstrahlung process (German translation = “braking energy”) is the process by which most x-rays are generated. Bremsstrahlung x-rays are produced when high-speed incident electrons interact with atomic nuclei. Repulsion by the positively charged atomic nuclei cause the electrons to slow down (hence the name "braking energy") and/or change directions. Some of the kinetic energy lost by the electrons in these interactions may be emitted as x-ray photons. Bremsstrahlung interactions result in polyenergetic x-rays. Because the force of attraction between the electrons and the nucleus varies inversely with distance squared, the magnitude of energy lost varies, and a polyenergetic x-ray beam is produced.

question_text: Increasing the tube voltage above the K-edge of the imaged tissue will have what effect on Compton scatter and photoelectric interactions?
choices: A. Compton interactions increase and photoelectric interactions decrease
B. Compton interactions decrease and photoelectric interactions increase
explanation: Both Compton and photoelectric interactions are inversely proportional to the energy of the incident x-ray photons. Thus, when the tube voltage is increased, there are less Compton interactions (1/E) and a lot less photoelectric (1/E‘3). This is why higher energy x-ray beams are more penetrating (not absorbed by the imaged tissue).

question_text: Compared to screen-film systems used in chest radiography, the latitude of mammographic screen- film systems is:
explanation: The film used in mammography has NARROWER latitude (i.e. range of exposures that can be used to create the image), which allows for BETTER contrast.

question_text: What is an electron volt (eV)?
choices: A. Potential energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated across an electric potential of 1 volt
B. Kinetic energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated across an electric potential of 1 volt
C. Kinetic energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated across an electric potential of 1 kilovolt
D. Potential energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated across an electric potential of 1 kilovolt
correct_answer_text: B. Kinetic energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated across an electric potential of 1 volt
explanation: One electron volt is defined as the kinetic energy gained by an electron when it is accelerated across an electric potential of 1 volt.

question_text: Tube loading, or the heat energy generated during an x-ray exposure, is dependent upon all of the following except:
explanation: Tube loading is dependent upon tube current, tube voltage, and exposure time.

question_text: Which of the following is not a function performed by an x-ray generator?
choices: A. Increase the voltage applied across an x-ray tube
B. Allow control of current within the x-ray tube
C. Rectify the wall current from direct current to alternating current
D. Allow control of the duration of time that current flows within an x-ray tube
correct_answer_text: C. Rectify the wall current from direct current to alternating current
explanation: X-ray generators rectify wall current from alternating current to direct current, not visa versa.

question_text: If the receptor air kerma is increased such that 400x more photons hit the detector, what happens to quantum mottle?
choices: A. Decrease by a factor of 20
B. Increase by a factor of 400
C. Increase by a factor of 20
E. Decrease by a factor of 400
correct_answer_text: A. Decrease by a factor of 20
explanation: Quantum mottle, or random noise, varies inversely as the square root of the mean number of photons incident on the detector. In this setting, it will decrease by the square root of 400, or 20.

question_text: Which of the following exams has the lowest entrance air kerma?
explanation: The PA chest radiograph will have the LOWEST entrance air kerma. Entrance air kerma is simply the amount of radiation that you need to shoot at a patient to obtain an adequate image. Depending on the size of the patient and the attenuation of the body part that you are image, the entrance air kerma may increase or decrease to keep the number of photons reaching the imaging receptor (image receptor dose) constant. Therefore, this question is simply asking which body part and/or projection will cause the least attenuation of the beam. The abdomen has a greater amount of soft tissue than the chest and will be associated with greater beam attenuation. The bony skull has a higher average attenuation coefficient than the tissues of the chest and will be associated with greater beam attenuation. Because of this, the chest radiograph will require less incident radiation to adequately penetrate the patient. The PA chest radiograph requires less incident radiation than the lateral chest radiograph because the width of the tissue to be penetrated is less in the PA dimension.

question_text: What is the spatial resolution of an image with full width half maximum (FWHM) of 0.4 mm?
explanation: Spatial resolution = 1/(2 x FWHM)

question_text: Approximately what percentage of incident x-ray photons are scattered out of a patient in plain film radiography?
explanation: Approximately 1/3 of incident x-ray photons are scattered out of a patient in plain film radiography. Compton scatter is the primary source of scatter in radiography. Photoelectric effect is the primary source of contrast in radiography.

question_text: Switching from a large focal spot to a small focal spot will require tube power.
explanation: The large 1.2mm focal spot can tolerate 100kW of power (the approximate power used for plain film radiography). In contrast, the smaller focal spot (0.6mm) can only tolerate 25kW of power. Thus, if you decrease the focal spot size, then you also need to decrease the power of your x-ray tube generator. The price of getting a sharper image from a smaller focal spot is that you have to use less power, which means more noise.

question_text: If the x-ray tube voltage is increased by a factor of 15%, which of the following best approximates the effect on the x-ray beam intensity?
choices: A. Decreased by a factor of 2
B. Increased by a factor of 4
C. Decreased by a factor of 4
D. Increased by a factor of 2
correct_answer_text: D. Increased by a factor of 2
explanation: To a first approximation, x-ray beam intensity increases as the square of the increase in tube voltage. Simply remember: increasing the kVp by 15% has a similar effect on the number of photons hitting the image receptor as doubling the mAs. Remember that increasing tube voltage changes both the quantity (i.e. beam intensity) and quality (i.e. energy) of an x-ray beam. To maintain constant exposure or dose when decreasing kVp: decrease kVp 15% and double mAs.

question_text: Which one of the following statements regarding absorbed radiation dose is true?
choices: A. Low atomic number material such as fat have absorbed dose < high atomic number material such as bone
B. Absorbed dose is independent of the tissue composition
C. Absorbed dose is measured in roentgens
D. Increasing the average x-ray beam energy increases the absorbed dose
correct_answer_text: A. Low atomic number material such as fat have absorbed dose < high atomic number material such as bone
explanation: [A]bsorbed dose is measured in gr[a]y or r[a]Jd. The absorbed dose depends on physical parameters such as density and average atomic number. Materials with a lower atomic number such as fat will absorb less radiation dose than materials with a higher atomic number such as bone. For some materials, increasing the average x-ray beam energy can reduce the absorbed dose, as more x-rays penetrate the material without being absorbed.

question_text: X-ray photons produced by Rayleigh scatter (coherent scatter) have _ than incident x-ray photons.
explanation: In Rayleigh scatter (coherent scatter), an incident x- ray photon collides with an electron causing it to vibrate. The electron subsequently emits an x-ray photon of the SAME energy as the incident x-ray. Therefore, the emitted x-ray has the SAME energy, wavelength, and frequency as the incident photon. Note that unlike photoelectric effect and Compton scatter, Rayleigh scatter does NOT produce an ionization event. Rayleigh scatter primarily occurs at low x-ray energies and makes up less than 5% of the incident x-ray interactions in the setting of radiography.

question_text: What happens to the half value layer if the tube current is increased while the tube voltage is held constant?
explanation: The half value layer is independent of tube current. In contrast, the half value layer of a substance will increase if the tube voltage is increased.

question_text: In which clinical situation is copper filtration of x-ray tube most likely to be used?
explanation: Both pediatric radiography and interventional radiology commonly use additional copper filtration. Copper filtration is used to reduce the radiation dose in pediatric imaging and to reduce the risk of skin burns in interventional radiology.

question_text: Which one of the following statements regarding x- ray scatter radiation is true?
choices: A. Most antiscatter grids are kept stationary during an exposure, to minimize image blurring
B. Introducing an air gap between the patient and the image receptor increases scatter
C. Introducing an air gap between the patient and the image receptor results in magnification
D. The best way to reduce scatter is to reduce the x-ray tube voltage
correct_answer_text: C. Introducing an air gap between the patient and the image receptor results in magnification
explanation: Introducing an air gap between the patient and the image receptor will result in magnification. This technique is used to achieve magnification in mammographic imaging. Note that while reducing x- ray tube voltage increases photoelectric absorption events and decreases Compton scatter, it also increases dose, and so it is not the best way to reduce x-ray scatter. Most antiscatter grids move during an exposure so that the grid lines are not visible on the image.

question_text: The largest volume within an atom is occupied by:
explanation: The nucleus and electrons are small as compared to the atom. Neutrinos and positrons do not exist in the atom under normal circumstances.

question_text: Which photon interaction delivers the least dose to the patient?
explanation: Coherent scatter (Rayleigh) photon interaction delivers the least dose to the patient. In coherent scattering interactions, the entire energy of the incoming photon is re-emitted in a different direction, resulting in zero energy transferred to the patient. In Compton scatter interactions, some photon energy is transferred to the patient and in photoelectric interactions, all of the photon energy is transferred to the patient. Bremsstrahlung is a process by which x-rays are produced when electrons are accelerated into an anode target.

question_text: An x-ray tube's voltage is increased from 65 to 75 kVp. What adjustment to the mAs should be made to achieve the same amount of radiation exposure to the image detector?
explanation: The 15% change in kVp Rule of Thumb (i.e. 65 kVp to 75 kVp is 15%) states that the number of photons reaching a detector would be doubled; therefore to compensate the operator needs to reduce the mAs by 1/2.

question_text: Which of the following would make the heel effect more pronounced?
choices: C. Larger apparent focal spot size
explanation: As the anode angle is reduced, the amount of material the x-rays must travel thought to exit the anode increases; this increases the heel effect by having more attenuation occur on the anode side of the radiation field of view. A large apparent focal spot means the anode angle is large minimizing the heel effect and only using a portion of the field of view allows the appearance of the heel effect at the detector to be less pronounced. A rotating anode has no effect on the heel effect.

question_text: Which of the following determines the maximum energy of Bremsstrahlung x-rays produced in an x- ray tube operated at 120 kVp?
choices: B. Peak voltage applied between the cathode and anode
C. Whether the generator is single or three phase
D. Product of the mA and exposure time
correct_answer_text: B. Peak voltage applied between the cathode and anode
explanation: The peak voltage applied between the cathode and anode determines the energy imparted to the electrons in the x-ray tube. It is this kinetic energy of the accelerated electrons that determines the maximum energy that can be produced. The other options all modify the amount of Bremsstrahlung radiation produced, not the energy.

question_text: Which of the following determines the energy of characteristic x-rays in an x-ray tube operated at 120 kVp?
choices: B. Whether the generator is single or three phase
C. Product of the mA and exposure time
D. Peak voltage applied between the cathode and
explanation: The type of target material used determines the energies of the characteristic radiation that can be produced based on its atomic number. Although the applied voltage must be large enough to provide the kinetic energy to the accelerated electron that is above the binding energies to produce characteristic x-rays, this does not "determine" the characteristic energies.

question_text: What is the effect of increasing the apparent focal spot size?
choices: B. Increased amount of heat the anode can absorb
correct_answer_text: B. Increased amount of heat the anode can absorb
explanation: By increasing the focal spot size, the amount of heat the anode can absorb is increased. Decreasing the focal spot size allows the geometric distortion at the image detector to be improved (i.e. spatial resolution). The focal spot size has no effect relative to image contrast or patient dose.

question_text: The X-ray grid improves image quality primarily by removing which of the following?
explanation: Compton scattering can change a photon's direction greatly; therefore, the grid can reduce the transmission of these photons significantly and improve image quality. The photoelectric effect absorbs photons, but does not scatter them. Backscattered photons would come from behind the image receptor, and would not be affected by the grid. Characteristic x-ray photons are affected by the grid exactly like Bremsstrahlung photons. Coherent scattering changes a photon's direction only a few degrees, and would be not be greatly affected by the grid.

question_text: Scatter-removing grids are least useful in magnification studies because:
choices: A. Small area of exposed tissue generates less scatter
B. Proper optical density could not be obtained
C. Small focal spot would resolve the grid lines too easily
D. Scatter reaching the film is reduced by the air gap
E. Not as much scatter is produced because of the small focal spot
correct_answer_text: D. Scatter reaching the film is reduced by the air gap
explanation: The large air gap reduces the fraction of scatter reaching the detector, greatly reducing the need for the grid. The focal spot size affects spatial resolution, but not the production of scatter. The grid is close enough to the image receptor that the focal spot size should have no influence on the production of grid line artifacts. Even though the area of exposed breast is smaller than in contact mammography, the x-ray intensity is greater closer to the focal spot, so exactly the same amount of scatter is produced.

question_text: On a per image basis, the lowest radiation exposure occurs with which of the following?
explanation: A head CT scan produces the largest exposure, followed by a chest radiograph, followed by a single view mammogram, and finally a single fluoroscopic image.

question_text: What is the effect of lowering kVp on radiation dose when other scan parameters remain unchanged?
explanation: While kVp controls the energy of x-ray photons, it also affects the quantity of x-rays produced. The change in radiation dose is approximately proportional to the square of the change in kVp. This is not true if mAs is adjusted to compensate for the decreased kVp -- dose is increased with lower kVp.

question_text: Where does radiation-induced cataract begin in the lens?
choices: A. Anterior pole of the lens
C. Posterior pole of the lens
D. Superior pole of the lens
correct_answer_text: C. Posterior pole of the lens
explanation: Radiation-induced cataract begins as a small dot in the posterior pole of the lens, differentiating it from senile cataracts, which start in the anterior pole of the lens.

question_text: The prodromal phase of acute radiation syndrome (ARS) includes:
explanation: The 4 phases of acute radiation sickness include the prodromal phase, latent phase, manifest illness and recovery or death. The prodromal phase is the first phase characterized by nausea, vomiting, anorexia, and/or diarrhea.

question_text: One week after a mammogram, a woman discovers that she is pregnant and estimates that the fetus is in its fourth week of gestation. Which of the following is the most appropriate response regarding the risks of her radiation exposure?
choices: A. The fetus is unharmed because there was no exposure.
B. The fetus received a minimal dose of scattered radiation. The risk for any biological effect is very low.
C. There is a high likelihood of a radiation-induced congenital abnormality
D. The child will have a 1% risk of being severely mentally retarded as a result of the radiation exposure.
correct_answer_text: B. The fetus received a minimal dose of scattered radiation. The risk for any biological effect is very low.
explanation: The fetus received a minimal dose of scattered radiation. The risk for any biological effect is very low.

question_text: Which study has the highest effective radiation?
explanation: Whole body PET/CT results in the greatest effective dose (approximately 10 mSv for the CT and 15 mSv for the PET) followed by Cardiac CTA (10-20 mSv), Chest CT (5-7 mSv) and finally Head CT (1-2 mSv). Remember the effective dose takes into account organ sensitivities.

question_text: Which low dose extrapolation model is used when setting radiation dose limits?
explanation: The most conservative model (linear, no threshold) is used by regulatory agencies when determining public and occupational radiation dose limits. This model assumes that any amount of radiation could potentially result in a stochastic effect (cancer) and that the probability of this effect is linearly related to dose.

question_text: No deterministic effects from radiation have been proven below which dose level?
explanation: No deterministic effects have been proven below 0.1 Gy. Because almost all diagnostic radiology exams administer a dose of less 0.1 Gy, it is very unlikely that one will encounter deterministic effects from a single diagnostic imaging study. However, if patients receive multiple diagnostic radiology studies or undergo long fluoroscopy studies, cumulative radiation dose can exceed 0.1 Gy

question_text: Which phase of the cell cycle are cells most radiosensitive?
explanation: Cells are most radiosensitive during mitosis or cell division and least radiosensitive during the S-phase or DNA synthesis phase.

question_text: The radiosensitivity of cells is directly proportional to which of the following?
explanation: Radiosensitivity is directly proportional to reproductive activity and inversely proportional to the degree of cellular differentiation according to the Law of Bergonie and Tirbondeau. Resistant cells include: cartilage, bone, liver, nervous tissue, kidney and muscle. Sensitive cells include epithelium of Gl tract, hematopoietic tissues, basal cells, germinal cells and lymphoid tissues.

question_text: Which radiation induced cancer has the shortest latency period?
explanation: Leukemia has the shortest latency period (5-15 years) whereas solid tumors have longer latency periods (20-60 years).

question_text: Fetal radiation induced malformations occur during what stage of gestation?
explanation: Radiation induced malformations occur during organogenesis (week 2-8) but are unlikely at doses less than 0.15 Gy which is lower than nearly all diagnostic exams.

question_text: Which of the following describes temporary male sterility?
choices: A. Deterministic effect with a threshold of 1 Gy
B. Deterministic effect with a threshold of 10 Gy
C. Deterministic effect with a threshold of 0.1 Gy
correct_answer_text: C. Deterministic effect with a threshold of 0.1 Gy
explanation: For board-style acute gonadal dose thresholds: temporary male sterility occurs at 0.1-0.15 Gy, while permanent male sterility occurs at 3.5-6 Gy.

question_text: Gamma rays and x-rays produce biologic effects through which mechanism?
choices: B. Indirectly by creating free radicals
D. Indirectly by creating radioactive isotopes
correct_answer_text: B. Indirectly by creating free radicals
explanation: Gamma rays and X-rays are examples of ionizing radiation, which creates free radicals from the ionization of water molecules. Free radicals can result in direct damage to DNA. Typically, the DNA can repair this damage. If the DNA cannot be repaired, cells either die or are left with a mutation which in some instances can lead to cancer.

question_text: The Bragg ionization peak indicates that most of the radiation dose from an x-ray tube without filtration is absorb by what organ?
explanation: With diagnostic x-ray energies, the skin surface receives the greatest dose.

question_text: The lethal dose in 50% of humans in 60 days is referred to as LD50/60 and occurs at what level of radiation?
explanation: The LD50/60 is somewhere between 3.5-7 Gy depending on level of medical care following exposure.

question_text: Skin erythema, cataracts and infertility are examples of?
explanation: These are example of deterministic effects. Deterministic effects have a radiation dose threshold. Above the threshold, the effect will occur and below the threshold, the effect will not occur. The severity of the effect does depend on dose.

question_text: Stochastic effects are more likely to occur with __—s?
explanation: Stochastic (probabilistic) effects are more likely to occur with increasing dose. There is no minimum threshold and the severity of the effect is independent of dose. Stochastic effects include cancer and genetic defects.

question_text: The carcinogenesis risk to which of the following organs is greatest for a given radiation dose?
explanation: Of the organs listed, the breast is thought to be most radiosensitive followed by the gonads, followed by thyroid and finally followed by skin. Although the skin gets a large dose, it is relatively less radiosensitive.

question_text: What is the occupational annual dose equivalent limit to the lens of the eye of an adult worker?
explanation: The NRC-mandated annual limit for lens dose equivalent is 150 mSv. However, note that the NRC is currently considering lowering this limit to 20 mSv per year based on the recommendations of the International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP). The ICRP recommends that the occupational exposure to the lens of the eye should be no greater than 20 mSv per year when averaged over 5 years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv.

question_text: Which of the following deterministic effects occurs as the lowest skin entrance dose?
explanation: Early transient erythema occurs at a skin entrance dose of 2Gy and can be seen starting 24 h after the exposure. Temporary and permanent epilation occur at skin entrance doses of 3 and 7Gy, respectively. Epilation can be seen starting 3 weeks after the exposure. Dry and moist desquamation occur at skin entrance doses of 10 and 15Gy, respectively. Desquamation can be seen starting 4 weeks after the exposure. Dermal atrophy occurs at a skin entrance dose of 10Gy. It has 2 phases, the first starting 14 weeks after the exposure and the second over a year after exposure.

question_text: Fetal absorbed doses of greater than occurring during of gestation may result in fetal death.
explanation: Fetal absorbed doses of greater than 100 mGy occurring during first two weeks of gestation may result in fetal death.

question_text: Leakage radiation in x-ray generation is defined as the amount of radiation that passes through the tube housing. Leakage radiation should not exceed which of the following values?
choices: A. 10 mGy/hr at 1 m from the source
B. 1.0 mGy/hr at 1 m from the source
C. 100 mGy/hr at 1 m from the source
D. 0.1 mGy/hr at 1 m from the source
correct_answer_text: B. 1.0 mGy/hr at 1 m from the source
explanation: Leakage should not exceed 1.0 mGy per hour at 1 m from the source.

question_text: Regulatory requirements mandate that diagnostic radiography units operated at tube potentials above 70 kVp must contain a total filtration (which includes the tube wall and any other added filtration) of at least how much of aluminum equivalent?
explanation: 2.5 mm of aluminum equivalent. At 80 keV, 2.5 mm of aluminum equivalent will attenuate the x-ray beam by approximately 50%.

question_text: A fetal absorbed dose of 10 mGy during the first trimester may result in a increase in the risk of childhood malignancy.
explanation: A fetal absorbed dose of 10 mGy during the first trimester may result in a 3.5 times increase in the likelihood of a childhood cancer. The risk goes from 0.07% to 0.25%.

question_text: What is the average total effective dose equivalent of radiation exposure received by a person in the U.S. annually?
explanation: The average person in the U.S. is exposed to approximately 6.2 mSv annually. Half of the exposure is from natural exposure (RADON, solar, etc.) and half is from medical exposure (mainly from CT and nuclear medicine studies).

question_text: When should termination of a pregnancy be considered after fetal radiation exposure?
choices: A. Fetal absorbed doses >50 mGy during the first 15 weeks of gestation
B. Fetal absorbed doses >50 mGy between 2nd - 15th weeks of gestation
C. Any fetal absorbed doses >50 mGy during pregnancy
correct_answer_text: B. Fetal absorbed doses >50 mGy between 2nd - 15th weeks of gestation
explanation: Study data suggests that termination should ONLY be considered for fetal absorbed doses >50 mGy that occur between the 2nd to 15th weeks of gestation. Consideration of termination is NOT appropriate for diagnostic radiation dose received in the first 2 weeks of gestation or after the 15th week of gestation, no matter what the dose. Consideration of termination is also not appropriate for doses <50 mGy that occur between the 2nd to 15th weeks of gestation.

question_text: What is threshold for deterministic skin effects?
explanation: Early transient erythema is the radiation-induced skin injury that occurs at the lowest skin dose. It occurs as a skin entrance dose of 2 Gy. Skin entrance doses <2 Gy are NOT associated with radiation-induced skin injury.

question_text: Which of the following is not associated with 130 mGy fetal absorbed dose?
explanation: Fetal absorbed doses of >100 mGy are associated: fetal death in the first 2 weeks of gestation, congenital malformations from 3rd - 8th week of gestation, growth retardation or impaired mental facility from 9th - 15th week. That radiation dose would also be associated with an increased risk of childhood malignancy. A 10 mGy fetal absorbed dose during the first trimester can result in a 3.5x increase in the likelihood of a childhood cancer. The increased risk of cancer would be even greater for this large radiation dose, given the stochastic nature of radiation-induced cancer risk.

question_text: In the US, which imaging modalities account for most annual amount of medical radiation?
explanation: In the US, CT provides the greatest dose to the general population followed by nuclear medicine studies. Together, they account for 50% of the imaging dose in the US.

question_text: The dose rate limit for an unrestricted area is:
choices: D. 0.02 mSv in a given hour
correct_answer_text: D. 0.02 mSv in a given hour
explanation: 0.02 mSv in a given hour

question_text: The annual radiation dose limit to the total body of adult occupational workers in the United States is currently:
explanation: 50 mSv

question_text: Based on the ICRP and NCRP recommendations, what is the annual dose limit for the lens of the eye for a member of the general public?
explanation: 15 mSv/yr for the general public. 150 mSv/yr for radiation worker.

question_text: What is the most sensitive conception age for radiation exposure inducing IQ deficits or mental retardation in-utero?
explanation: 9th to 15th weeks. Radiation effects are a concern from the 3rd to the 15th weeks conception age, with the neurological effects occurring later.

question_text: Which exam has the lowest average Relative Radiation Level?
explanation: Tc99m Lung Perfusion. Most cardiac studies are high radiation dose, while nuclear medicine lung perfusion studies are not that high.

question_text: Which tissue weighting factor is the lowest?
explanation: Skin. All of other tissues have a high cellular turnover.

question_text: What is the term for tissue damage from radiation exposure that results in cell death?
explanation: Cell death is a deterministic effect by definition.

question_text: During coronary angioplasty, the patient received a surface dose of 4.5 Gy. What are the potential deterministic effects that may occur in this patient?
explanation: Skin Erythema. Since neither the eye nor abdomen should be in the FOV, these tissues would receive only scatter radiation from the exam. Therefore, the dose to these regions would be only a fraction of the surface dose, whereas the skin receives the entire surface dose.

question_text: Gamma rays can be shielded using:
choices: C. Aluminum, wood, glass or plastic material
explanation: Gamma particles must be shielded by using high Z materials such as lead; concrete can be used as building material and give the possibility to construct wide thickness walls with reasonable cost.

question_text: According to BEIR report VII, what is the average risk of mortality from radiation-induced cancer in the general population?
explanation: 5% per Sv is the average risk of mortality from radiation-induced cancer in the general population.

question_text: The average CT examination of an abdomen/pelvis would be equal to how many years of natural background radiation in the United States?
explanation: The average CT examination of an abdomen/pelvis is about 9 mSv. This is equal to 3 years of natural background radiation (3 mSv/year) in the United States.

question_text: Chest radiography offers which of the following distinct advantages over CT?
explanation: Plain films have the best spatial resolution followed by ultrasound, CT, MR and finally NM.

question_text: Why are abdominal x-ray images acquired at 70 kVp rather than at 120 kVp?
explanation: Decreasing kVp results in increased soft tissue x- ray attenuation which allows small differences in soft tissue density to be resolved, i.e. better contrast resolution. This principle also explains why dose decreases as kVp increases, i.e. there’s less tissue absorption and more penetration.

question_text: If the vertebral bodies on a chest radiograph in the lower mediastinum are discernible but the lungs appear too white, what should be adjusted?
explanation: If the vertebral bodies are discernible in the lower mediastinum, the chest radiograph is properly penetrated (kVp is high enough). Therefore, the problem lies with exposure which is determined by the number of X-rays reaching the image receptor (mAs).

question_text: Grids should be used when imaging which of the following?
explanation: Grids are effective in decreasing scatter radiation. Scatter increases as the amount of tissue that the x- ray beam must go through increases. Therefore grids are used when imaging thick body parts like the chest and abdomen. Because grids also attenuate the primary beam, they lead to increased dose and therefore are often not used to image pediatric patients or small body parts such as the extremities.

question_text: Which of the following is an appropriate x-ray tube voltage for a standard frontal chest radiograph?
explanation: 120 kV

question_text: For a digital radiograph, what effect will widening the display window width have on the display contrast?
explanation: Widening the display window will decrease the display contrast.

question_text: Which best approximates the power used to obtain an image with plain film radiography?
explanation: The approximate power used for plain film radiography is 100kW. The approximate power used for mammography is 3kW.

question_text: What is the approximate entrance air kerma for an abdominal radiograph?
explanation: Entrance air kerma is the radiation dose measured in the air just before entering the skin surface. The approximate entrance air kerma for an abdominal radiograph is 3 mGy.

question_text: If the average entrance air kerma for an abdominal radiograph is 3 mGy, what is the approximate entrance skin dose for an abdominal radiograph?
explanation: The entrance skin dose tends to be 50% more than the entrance air kerma. Therefore, the estimated skin dose would be about 5 mGy. Estimated skin doses for common radiographic projections vary from 0.2 mGy for a PA chest to 15 mGy for a lateral lumbar spine.

question_text: Which of the following statements regarding image resolution in film radiography is correct?
choices: A. Thicker screens result in less screen blurring than thinner screens
B. Shorter exposure times result in improved spatial resolution compared to longer exposure times
C. Thicker screens require longer exposure times
D. Screen greatly improves spatial resolution
correct_answer_text: B. Shorter exposure times result in improved spatial resolution compared to longer exposure times
explanation: Ascreen in film radiography converts x-ray photon to visible light photon, which then exposes the film. Screens reduces spatial resolution due to diffusion of light photons within the screen. The thicker the screen, the greater the diffusion and blur effect. Shorter exposure times minimize the effect of patient motion and thus result in improved spatial resolution. Thicker screens have higher absorption efficiency and can therefore accommodate shorter exposure times.

question_text: In screen film radiography with automatic exposure control, if the thickness of the screen is increased, which of the following best describes the impact on quantum mottle?
explanation: The amount of quantum mottle is directly related to the number of x-ray photons detected by the image receptor. Thicker screens improve x-ray photon absorption efficiency (i.e., increase the number of x- ray photons detected), but as a result require shorter exposure times (i.e., decrease the number of x-ray photons detected) when automatic exposure control is on. Thus, the net effect is an unchanged number of light photons that are incident on the receptor.

question_text: Which one of the following statements regarding x- ray digital detectors is true?
choices: A. Direct flat panel detectors use photoconducting materials such as selenium
B. Indirect flat panel detectors use photostimulable phosphors to store x-ray beam information, which is subsequently read out by a laser
C. In contrast to direct radiography (DR), conventional radiography (CR) allows for a shorter time between image acquisition and image display
D. The absorption efficiency of x-rays by selenium increases with increasing average x-ray beam energy
correct_answer_text: A. Direct flat panel detectors use photoconducting materials such as selenium
explanation: Direct flat panel detectors use photoconducting materials such as selenium. Selenium’s absorption of x-rays is better at low x-ray energies due to the low K-shell binding energy. Indirect flat panel detectors use scintillators to emit light photons that are then immediately detected and converted to an image. Direct radiography allows for near immediate display of images, whereas conventional radiography requires the image to be processed.

question_text: An infant requires repeated chest x-rays. Which of these is worth considering to minimize dose while providing highest image quality?
choices: A. Use of small focal spot
C. Use of lowest possible kVp (kVp < 40)
D. Use of highest possible kVp (kVp > 120)
correct_answer_text: A. Use of small focal spot
explanation: Using the small focal spot will provide the highest image quality through superior resolution, while maintaining dose (since the focal spot selection does not impact patient dose). A kVp less than 40 will increase patient dose. A kVp greater than 120 will not provide adequate contrast for such a small patient. Use of the grid will increase patient dose, and because infants are so small in size, the scatter they create is much less than adult patients and the use of a grid is often not necessary.

question_text: A good strategy in imaging large patients while minimizing radiation dose includes which one of the following?
choices: A. Use of small focal spots
explanation: A good strategy would be to increase the kVp. A "hardened" beam will better penetrate a large patient at reduced dose. Increasing the mAs will increase patient dose. Using a larger SID will require a higher output on the x-ray tube (due to beam divergence and loss of intensity with distance) and could lead to increased exposure time and therefore patient motion. A small focal spot is not optimal for large patients due to limitations of tube output, increased exposure time and potential for patient motion.

question_text: A trauma patient has been brought to the ER She is obese and pregnant. You need to get a chest radiograph. What is be the best course of action to get the needed information without overexposure to the fetus?
choices: A. Use grid with standard kVp and tight collimation
B. Obtain low kVp image without grid
C. Use high kVp with low mAs
D. Use low kVp and mAs
correct_answer_text: A. Use grid with standard kVp and tight collimation
explanation: The best course of action would to use a grid to clean up the increased scatter resulting from the large patient size, tight collimation to reduce dose to the fetus, and a standard kVp will provide adequate image contrast. A low kVp might not penetrate an obese patient well enough for adequate image quality, and by not using a grid, the scattered radiation would degrade image quality. A high kVp will degrade image contrast.

question_text: Which of the following is an advantage of film/screen systems over digital x-ray imaging systems?
choices: D. Appearance of the image indicates it was properly exposed
correct_answer_text: D. Appearance of the image indicates it was properly exposed
explanation: Digital image receptors have a much wider dynamic range than film/screen cassettes. Patient throughput is similar for digital and film/screen systems. Digital images have many more image processing options than film images, whose characteristics become fixed when the images are developed. Digital imaging systems generally require fewer retakes than film/screen systems, because the increased dynamic range allows acceptable images to be produced even when the detector is moderately overexposed or underexposed. If a film is overexposed or underexposed, it will be darker or lighter than a properly exposed film. Digital systems automatically compensate for exposure errors through image processing. Note that this can lead to "dose creep", in which technologists use higher and higher dose techniques to produce less noisy images. In general, screen-film radiography has better image spatial resolution.

question_text: The goal of dual-energy subtraction chest radiography is to:
choices: B. Separate vessels from tissue and bone structures
C. Achieve a low noise image
D. Obtain a tissue only image and a bone only image
correct_answer_text: D. Obtain a tissue only image and a bone only image
explanation: A dual-energy imaging system converts a high energy and a low energy image to a pair of material images, typically bone and soft tissue. When the two images are taken in sequence, dual-energy systems are more susceptible to motion artifacts. Blood has a similar elemental composition to soft tissue, so they are present in the soft tissue image. The material images are typically noisier than the original images.

question_text: What is a feature of dual-source CT?
choices: A. Two x-ray sources with single detector array
explanation: Dual-source CT scanners consist of 2 x-ray tubes & 2 detector arrays, with each x-ray tube's kVp + mAs set independently. Since a material's linear attenuation coefficient varies with the kVp used, two different linear attenuation coefficients are obtained for the same tissue. Virtual unenhanced images can be generated from a single scan with contrast. With two x-ray sources, there is increased temporal resolution (fast scan times), which is useful in imaging fast-moving anatomy such as in cardiac imaging.

question_text: In CT, the table distance traveled in one complete rotation divided by the width of the x-ray beam is described by which of the following terms?
explanation: Pitch = table distance traveled in one 360-degree gantry rotation divided by beam collimation

question_text: In CT imaging, spatial resolution improves when which of the following increases?
explanation: Pixel size = FOV/matrix size. If matrix size is increased, pixel size decreases and spatial resolution increases. A small pixel size means fewer photons/pixel leading to increased noise and therefore decreased contrast resolution.

question_text: What parameter change increases CT radiation dose?
explanation: Increasing current (mAs) results in more x-rays photons leaving the x-ray tube and contributing to image acquisition, thereby decreasing noise. Decreased noise is of course at the expense of increased dose. In general, kVp can be controlled independent of mAs.

question_text: What do CTDIvol and DLP reported on CT scanners represent?
explanation: CTDIvol is measured from phantoms placed in a scanner under specific settings (kVp, mAs, pitch, etc). DLP is calculated from CTDIvol and scan length.

question_text: Increasing slice thickness by raw data reconstruction results in:
explanation: Increasing slice thickness by reconstructing the raw data results in decreased noise (i.e. more photons per voxel), increased volume averaging artifact, and decreased resolution in the z-axis. Dose does not change as reconstruction is a form of post processing.

question_text: Automatic Exposure Control in a CT scanner adjusts the x-ray tube output using real-time calculated attenuation values. The use of breast shields may result in which of the following?
choices: A. Increased signal to noise ratio
D. Decreased tube output over the breasts
explanation: If AEC adjusts its output based on real-time attenuation measurements, then the x-ray tube will ramp up tube output (mA or kVp) when imaging over the shielding device leading to increased dose. It's important to understand the AEC system in place for each scanner to avoid this pitfall.

question_text: What is the CTDIvol for a pitch of 1, surface dose of 36 mGy and center dose of 12 mGy?
explanation: The CTDIvol is calculated for each scanner using a phantom and for specific imaging parameters (mA, kVp, etc). CTDIvol = 2/3 surface dose + 1/3 center dose = 2/3*36 + 1/3*12 = 28 mGy.

question_text: For a standard abdominal CT, what is the dose to the center of a patient's abdomen relative to the periphery?
explanation: The dose to the center of a patient's abdomen during a standard abdominal CT is approximately 1/2 the dose to the periphery. CTDIweighted = CTDicenter/3 + 2*CTDIperiphery/3. CTDIvol = CTDIw/pitch.

question_text: With Automatic Tube Current Modulation, the tube current is calculated using which of the following?
explanation: ATCM uses the scout radiographs to determine the expected mA needed to ensure noise is below an acceptable level.

question_text: Which of the following is increased with dual-energy CT scanners?
explanation: One of the main applications for dual energy CT scanners is cardiac imaging. Cardiac imaging requires superior temporal resolution.

question_text: Which of the following is increased with increasing slice thickness?
explanation: Increasing slice thickness results in more photons per voxel which decreases noise and increases contrast resolution. Larger voxels lead to decreased z-axis resolution.

question_text: A Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) of an imaging system describes which of the following?
choices: A. Spatial resolution in spatial domain
B. Contrast resolution in spatial domain
C. Spatial resolution in frequency domain
D. Contrast resolution in frequency domain
correct_answer_text: C. Spatial resolution in frequency domain
explanation: MTF of an imaging system describes its spatial resolution in the frequency domain. The MTF of a system is the product of a system's component MTFs.

question_text: What occurs when changing from a smoothing algorithm to a sharpening algorithm?
explanation: Image reconstruction using different algorithms/kernels occurs after the scan is complete and therefore have no impact on patient dose.

question_text: Cardiac functional information like ejection fraction can only be obtained with which of the following?
explanation: During prospective gating, the x-ray tube is only on during a short portion of the R-R interval (diastole) and therefore data is not acquired during the entire cardiac cycle. As result, functional information cannot be calculated. With retrospective gating, the x-ray tube is on during the entire cardiac cycle, allowing for images of the heart to be reconstructed at any point during the cardiac cycle. As a result, functional information can be calculated and there's higher radiation dose. Retrospective gating can only be performed with helical scanners.

question_text: What happens to the CT table during acquisition of the localizer/scout images?
choices: B. Moves in evenly spaced increments
explanation: When the scout image or localizer is being performed, the patient moves continuously through the gantry. The x-ray tube during this time is stationary. Although the scout or localizer looks like a plain film, the patient is moving through the gantry during its creation. Similarly, CT scanogram are commonly performed for scoliosis evaluation instead of multiple stitched radiographs.

question_text: What occurs with increased pitch?
explanation: Increasing pitch means that the patients moves a greater distance through the gantry per revolution of the x-ray tube. This results in decreased dose and a decrease in time exposed to the x-ray beam. Dose is related to 1/pitch. The decreased dose leads to an increase in noise and decrease in contrast resolution.

question_text: If the CTDIvol is 30 mGy, scan length is 5 cm and FOV is 10 cm, what is the Dose Length Product (DLP)?
explanation: DLP = CTDIvol * scan length. CTDIvol = CTDIweighted / pitch. FOV is a distracter in this question. DLP units are mGy*cm.

question_text: Which reconstruction algorithm should be used when evaluating for interstitial lung disease?
explanation: Because the lung and lung pathology already has high intrinsic contrast resolution and because visualizing ILD requires high spatial resolution, a high spatial resolution or sharpening algorithm is necessary.

question_text: A CT of the chest was obtained and reconstructed with a soft tissue algorithm. However, the radiologist wanted a sharp algorithm. What should be done?
choices: A. Scan the patient again with a sharp algorithm
B. Scan the patient again with lower kVp
C. Scan the patient again with lower mAs
D. Reconstruct the raw data with a sharp kernel
correct_answer_text: D. Reconstruct the raw data with a sharp kernel
explanation: The original raw data can be processed with one of many algorithms/kernels. In the chest, often a high spatial resolution reconstruction is desired. Therefore, a sharp algorithm or kernel is applied to the raw data. The raw data never changes (also patient dose) and can be reconstructed with as many different algorithms as are available or desired. The technologist or radiologist must simply reconstruct the raw data using a high resolution or sharp algorithm and send this reconstruction to PACs. Likewise, the radiologist can try to reconstruct the raw data with a larger or smaller FOV to include or exclude a structure. All scanners keep the raw data for a short amount of time (typically on the order of weeks) at which point reconstructions can no longer be performed.

question_text: When a smoothing, sharpening or noise reducing kernel/algorithm is applied, what happens to each voxel's linear attenuation coefficient?
choices: A. Relatively unchanged to other voxels
C. Relatively unchanged to adjacent voxels
explanation: Post processing at the view box with, for example, windowing and leveling does not change the actual CT numbers/linear attenuation coefficients. It only changes the way the data is displayed. However, a kernel or algorithm does change each voxel’s linear attenuation coefficient. Each filter or kernel applied results in a different linear attenuation coefficient for any individual voxel. In other words, kernels/algorithms change the raw data.

question_text: The Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) of a system is equal to which of the following?
choices: A. Median of the system's component MTFs
B. Sum of the system's component MTFs
C. Average of the system's component MTFs
D. Product of the system's component MTFs
correct_answer_text: D. Product of the system's component MTFs
explanation: The MTF of a system is the product of a system's component MTFs. A Modulation Transfer Function is a measure of a system or component's spatial resolution performance in the frequency domain.

question_text: A soft tissue reconstruction algorithm results in which of the following?
explanation: A soft tissue reconstruction algorithm decreases image noise leading to increased contrast resolution and imaging smoothing, which is more pleasing to the eye. Image smoothing decreases spatial resolution. Alternatively, a bone filter/sharpening algorithm increases spatial resolution at the expense of increased noise (decreased contrast resolution).

question_text: With Automatic Tube Current Modulation (ATCM), tube current increases when scanning through which direction?
explanation: Tube current increases when noise/beam attenuation is expected to be high based on the scout radiographs. Because there is little attenuation through the chest in the AP or PA projections, ATCM decreases mA at these positions. However, ATCM increases mA when the projection is through the lateral chest at the level of the shoulders in an effort to reduce noise created by the shoulders.

question_text: Which of the following improves CT spatial resolution?
choices: C. Decreasing number of ray sums
explanation: Spatial resolution in the z-axis is directly related to slice thickness. The smaller the slice, the better z- axis resolution. In addition, as the number of projections per slice or ray sums per slice increase the more data there is per slice to reconstruct the image, which results in less noise and better x-y plane resolution. Increasing the focal spot size results in more blur and decreased resolution.

question_text: How is the x-ray tube mA adjusted during CT fluoroscopy?
choices: A. Decreased to increase temporal resolution
explanation: During CT fluoroscopy, the x-ray tube mA is decreased to limit dose. The dose, in particular to the skin, can be significant because the same region is scanned over and over again. To minimize dose, at the cost of noise and therefore contrast resolution, mA is decreased.

question_text: Where is the most noise seen in a CT abdomen?
explanation: Relatively fewer lower energy x-rays make it to the patient's central abdomen than to the patient's peripheral abdomen. It is these lower energy x-rays that contribute to contrast. Therefore, there is less contrast and more noise in the center than at the periphery of a patient's abdomen. Similarly, the dose is greater at the periphery than at the center of a patient's abdomen.

question_text: Bow-tie filters are used to attenuated the x-ray beam more at the ?
explanation: Bow-tie filters account for patient anatomy and reduce photon intensity variations across the detector elements; therefore they attenuate the x- ray beam at the periphery where there is expected to be less patient attenuation. This creates a more uniform Signal to Noise Ratio (SNR). By optimizing the x-ray beam, bow tie filters result in decreased patient dose. Filters in CT scanners are typically composed of aluminum or copper. Bow tie filters are typically composed of materials with low atomic numbers such as Teflon and attenuate the x-ray beam maximally at its edges and minimally at its center.

question_text: Iterative reconstruction algorithms as compared to analytical reconstruction algorithms result in ?
explanation: Iterative reconstruction algorithms decrease quantum noise and allow for lower dose CT scans. Analytical reconstruction algorithms use filtered back projection which can be faster but result in more noise and streak artifacts.

question_text: A patient has two CT chest scans 3-months apart. The first scan reports the CTDIvol = 15 mGy. The second scan utilized a new tube modulation protocol and reports the CTDIvol = 10 mGy. Which of the following is true?
choices: A. Patient received 10 mGy during the second scan
B. Patient received an accumulative dose of 25 mGy
C. Patient received 1/3 the dose of the first scan
D. Patient received 2/3rd the dose of the first scan
correct_answer_text: D. Patient received 2/3rd the dose of the first scan
explanation: CTDIvol is a measurement made from phantoms placed into the scanner under predetermined conditions (specific kVp, mAs, pitch, etc). CTDIvol does not reflect the actual dose to the patient, i.e. the patient is not made of plastic. Relative changes in CTDlvol do reflect the relative changes in patient dose however. A change from 15 mGy to 10 mGy reflects a 33% decrease in dose and therefore the patient received 2/3rds of the original dose.

question_text: If a CT scan is viewed with a window width of 120 Hounsfield units (HU) and a window level of 50 HU, which of the following identifies the pixels that will appear white on the screen?
explanation: The window width identifies what the cutoff for black and white pixels are, based around a center line defined by the level. In this situation, with the center line at 50 HU, all pixels > 110 HU will be white and all pixels < -10 HU will be black.

question_text: If the CT table position moves 20mm for each rotation and the width of the x-ray beam is 5mm, what is the pitch?
explanation: Pitch = table distance traveled in one 360-degree gantry rotation divided by beam collimation

question_text: A CT scan is performed on a standard 32cm acrylic phantom. The CT dose index (CTDI) at the periphery of the phantom is 18mGy, and the CTDI at the center of the phantom is 6mGy. Which of the following best describes the weighted CTDI (CTDIw) for the scan?
explanation: CTDIw = 2/3 peripheral CTDI + 1/3 central CTDI = 14 mGy in this case. CTDIvol = CTDIw/pitch.

question_text: A CT scan is obtained with 1.5 mm slice thickness. If the data are reconstructed at 6 mm slice thicknesses, which of the following best describes the effect on the image mottle?
choices: A. Increased by a factor of 2
B. Increased by a factor of 4
C. Reduced by a factor of 2
E. Reduced by a factor of 4
correct_answer_text: C. Reduced by a factor of 2
explanation: Quadrupling the slice thickness quadruples the number of photons in a CT image. Mottle is inversely related to the square root of the number of photons, and thus it will be halved.

question_text: Which of the following statements regarding Bremsstrahlung radiation is correct?
choices: A. Bremsstrahlung radiation produces x-rays of discrete energies equal to the maximum kinetic energy of the incident, energetic electrons
B. Bremsstrahlung radiation decreases with increasing atomic number of the target
C. Bremsstrahlung radiation is produced when the incident, energetic electron interacts with atomic outer shell electrons
D. Bremsstrahlung radiation accounts for the majority of x-rays produced for most radiographic imaging systems
correct_answer_text: D. Bremsstrahlung radiation accounts for the majority of x-rays produced for most radiographic imaging systems
explanation: Bremsstrahlung radiation accounts for the majority of x-rays produced for most radiographic imaging systems. It is caused by electron interactions with atomic nuclei. It produces a spectrum of x-ray energies and increases with increasing atomic number.

question_text: Which of the following statements regarding CT radiation dose is correct?
choices: A. Doubled tube rotation time = doubled radiation dose
B. Doubled tube voltage (kV) = doubled radiation dose
C. Doubled pitch = doubled radiation dose
D. Doubled tube current (mA) = quadrupled radiation dose
correct_answer_text: A. Doubled tube rotation time = doubled radiation dose
explanation: Radiation dose in CT is linearly related to tube current (mA) and rotation time, but not to tube voltage (kV). Doubling the pitch will halve the radiation dose.

question_text: What range of peak kilovoltages (kVp) are typically used for CT imaging?
explanation: CT scanners typically operate at 120-140 kVp. However, lower voltage imaging around 80 kVp may be used to optimally visualize iodinated contrast. 25-30 kVp is the range for mammography. 80-100 kVp is the range for radiography, although chest radiographs are typically shot using a higher tube voltage of 120 kVp.

question_text: Which of the following is associated with beam hardening due to x-ray tube filters?
choices: A. Lower average x-ray beam energy
D. Loss of higher-energy photons from a polychromatic x-ray beam
explanation: Beam hardening is defined as the loss of low-energy photons from a polychromatic x-ray beam and may occur naturally or as the result of beam filtration. Beam hardening increases the average energy of the x-ray beam, which increases the beam's ability to penetrate tissue. However, since fewer overall photons make it through the filter to reach the patient, the x-ray tube output is decreased by the use of filtration.

question_text: Definitive diagnosis of macroscopic fat requires a CT attenuation value below what level?
explanation: While fat will often measure between -80 HU and -100 HU, a CT attenuation value below -30 HU is definitively diagnostic for macroscopic fat. A non- contrast CT attenuation < 10 HU = lipid-rich adrenal adenoma.

question_text: In a 128-detector row CT scanner with a 0.5mm detector width, what is the beam width?
explanation: In multidetector row CT, the beam width is equal to the number of detector rows multiplied by the detector width.

question_text: The mAs for a CT scan is increased from 80 to 320. Which of the following best describes the impact on the mottle?
choices: A. Decreased by a factor of 4
B. Decreased by a factor of 2
C. Increased by a factor of 4
D. Increased by a factor of 2
correct_answer_text: B. Decreased by a factor of 2
explanation: Mottle or noise is inversely related to the square root of the number of photons that are incident on the detector. The latter is in turn proportional to the mAs. Thus, quadrupling the mAs will halve the mottle or noise.

question_text: Which of the following will not reduce the quantum mottle?
choices: A. Repeat CT with a higher mAs
B. Change reconstruction algorithm to soft tissue/smooth algorithm
C. Reconstruct slices with increased slice thickness
D. Repeat CT with a lower kV
correct_answer_text: D. Repeat CT with a lower kV
explanation: Increasing slice thickness, using a higher mAs, using a higher kV, and using a soft tissue/smooth reconstruction algorithm will all result in decreased mottle.

question_text: If a CT scan is performed with a tube current of 500 mA, a tube voltage of 120 kV, and a rotation time of 0.1 sec, what is mAs?
explanation: mAs = tube current x rotation time. In this case, 500 mA x 0.1s = 50 mAs.

question_text: What is the relationship between pitch, scan time, and dose in CT imaging?
choices: A. Increasing pitch decreases dose and decreases scan time
B. Increasing pitch increases dose but decreases scan time
C. Increasing pitch does not affect dose but decreases scan time
D. Increasing pitch decreases dose but increases scan time
correct_answer_text: A. Increasing pitch decreases dose and decreases scan time
explanation: Increasing pitch decreases dose and decreases scan time. Pitch = table distance traveled in one 360-degree gantry rotation divided by beam collimation.

question_text: Which of the following statements is false regarding Hounsfield units (HU)?
choices: A. HU depends on the tube voltage (kV)
B. HU of pure water is 0
C. HU depends on the type of reconstruction algorithms used
D. HU are the same across CT scanners
correct_answer_text: D. HU are the same across CT scanners
explanation: Hounsfield units ARE dependent upon the kV of the examination and the type of filtration used. To understand why this is true, remember that Hounsfield units simply reflect the relative attenuation coefficient of a material compared to water. Since the attenuation coefficient of a material is dependent on photon energy (keV), this means that Hounsfield unit values are also dependent on photon energy (keV). Changing the tube voltage (kV) or using filtration will change the energy of the x-ray beam, therefore they will also change the Hounsfield unit values. By definition, the Hounsfield unit for water is 0. The use of different reconstruction algorithms, or ‘filters,’ also changes Hounsfield units; for example, at interfaces between two very different attenuators (i.e. bone and air), a high spatial resolution filter will have different HU compared to a smooth filter.

question_text: In CT imaging, body parts outside of the FOV receive radiation dose than those inside the FOV.
explanation: Unlike in radiography, in CT imaging reducing the FOV does not decrease dose to the tissues outside of the FOV.

question_text: What happens to the table during a non-helical axial CT?
explanation: During a non-helical axial CT scan, the table moves in discrete increments. At each location in the z- direction, the x-ray tube makes one complete revolution before turning off. The table then moves to the next location. This results in good z-axis resolution because only the data at each z location is used to construct the image. During a helical CT scan, the table moves continuously through a rotating x-ray tube. At each z-location, data at this location as well as above and below this location are used to reconstruct the image. This results in decreased z-axis resolution and increased partial volume averaging. The upside is that dose is decreased and scan times are much faster.
